concepts.name: steepest descent
concepts.description: An optimization method that chooses the increment vector as negative of the gradient times a scalar step size, moving in the direction of most rapid decrease.
concepts.quote: Steepest descent chooses hm = −ρmgm where ρm is a scalar and gm ∈ IRN is the gradient of L(f) evaluated at f = fm−1.

concepts.name: gradient boosting
concepts.description: A boosting technique that uses the gradient of the loss function to fit trees at each iteration, generalizing boosting to arbitrary differentiable loss functions.
concepts.quote: A possible resolution to this dilemma is to induce a tree T(x; Θm) at the mth iteration whose predictions tm are as close as possible to the negative gradient.

concepts.name: pseudo residuals
concepts.description: The negative gradient components in gradient boosting, also called generalized residuals, which serve as targets for fitting regression trees.
concepts.quote: The components of the negative gradient computed at line 2(a) are referred to as generalized or pseudo residuals, r.

concepts.name: right-sized trees for boosting
concepts.description: The practice of using trees of fixed size J in boosting, where J controls the level of interaction effects that can be captured.
concepts.quote: The simplest strategy for avoiding this problem is to restrict all trees to be the same size, Jm = J ∀m.

concepts.name: anova expansion
concepts.description: A decomposition of a function into main effects, second-order interactions, and higher-order interactions between variables.
concepts.quote: One relevant property of η(X) is the degree to which the coordinate variables interact with one another. This is captured by its ANOVA (analysis of variance) expansion.

concepts.name: main effect
concepts.description: The contribution of a single predictor variable to the target function, independent of interactions with other variables.
concepts.quote: The first sum in (10.40) is over functions of only a single predictor variable Xj. The particular functions ηj(Xj) are those that jointly best approximate η(X) under the loss criterion being used. Each such ηj(Xj) is called the 'main effect' of Xj.

concepts.name: decision stump
concepts.description: A tree with a single split (J=2), producing boosted models with only main effects and no interactions.
concepts.quote: Setting J = 2 (single split 'decision stump') produces boosted models with only main effects; no interactions are permitted.

concepts.name: early stopping
concepts.description: A regularization strategy where training is terminated before reaching the global minimum, monitoring validation error to prevent overfitting.
concepts.quote: A convenient way to estimate M* is to monitor prediction risk as a function of M on a validation sample. The value of M that minimizes this risk is taken to be an estimate of M*. This is analogous to the early stopping strategy often used with neural networks.

concepts.name: shrinkage in boosting
concepts.description: Scaling the contribution of each tree by a learning rate factor 0 < ν < 1 when added to the current approximation, controlling the learning rate of boosting.
concepts.quote: The simplest implementation of shrinkage in the context of boosting is to scale the contribution of each tree by a factor 0 < ν < 1 when it is added to the current approximation.

concepts.name: learning rate in boosting
concepts.description: The parameter ν controlling how much each tree contributes to the boosted model, with smaller values requiring more iterations but often yielding better test error.
concepts.quote: The parameter ν can be regarded as controlling the learning rate of the boosting procedure. Smaller values of ν (more shrinkage) result in larger training risk for the same number of iterations M.

concepts.name: stochastic gradient boosting
concepts.description: A variant of gradient boosting that samples a fraction of training observations at each iteration before growing the next tree, improving performance and computational efficiency.
concepts.quote: With stochastic gradient boosting (Friedman, 1999), at each iteration we sample a fraction η of the training observations (without replacement), and grow the next tree using that subsample.

concepts.name: subsampling in boosting
concepts.description: Sampling a fraction of training data at each boosting iteration to reduce computation time and often improve accuracy.
concepts.quote: Not only does the sampling reduce the computing time by the same fraction η, but in many cases it actually produces a more accurate model.

concepts.name: relative importance of predictor variables
concepts.description: A measure quantifying the relevance of each input variable in boosted tree models, based on squared improvements in the splitting criterion.
concepts.quote: For a single decision tree T, Breiman et al. (1984) proposed [the sum over internal nodes of squared improvements] as a measure of relevance for each predictor variable Xℓ.

concepts.name: partial dependence plots
concepts.description: Graphical visualization showing the marginal effect of a subset of predictor variables on the prediction function after accounting for the average effects of other variables.
concepts.quote: One way to define the average or partial dependence of f(X) on XS is the marginal average of f.

concepts.name: weak learner
concepts.description: An algorithm for producing a two-class classifier with performance guaranteed to be significantly better than random guessing.
concepts.quote: A weak learner is an algorithm for producing a two-class classifier with performance guaranteed (with high probability) to be significantly better than a coin-flip.

concepts.name: projection pursuit regression
concepts.description: A regression model that fits additive nonlinear functions of linear combinations (projections) of the inputs, where both the projection directions and ridge functions are estimated from data.
concepts.quote: The projection pursuit regression (PPR) model has the form f(X) = Σm gm(ωᵀmX), where ωm are unit p-vectors of unknown parameters and the functions gm are unspecified and estimated along with the directions.

concepts.name: ridge function
concepts.description: A function in p-dimensional space that varies only in the direction defined by a vector, of the form g(ωᵀX).
concepts.quote: The function gm(ωᵀmX) is called a ridge function in IRp. It varies only in the direction defined by the vector ωm.

concepts.name: universal approximator
concepts.description: A class of models that can approximate any continuous function arbitrarily well given sufficient complexity.
concepts.quote: In fact, if M is taken arbitrarily large, for appropriate choice of gm the PPR model can approximate any continuous function in IRp arbitrarily well. Such a class of models is called a universal approximator.

concepts.name: single index model
concepts.description: A projection pursuit regression model with M=1, known in econometrics, slightly more general than linear regression.
concepts.quote: The M = 1 model, known as the single index model in econometrics, is an exception. It is slightly more general than the linear regression model, and offers a similar interpretation.

concepts.name: gauss-newton search
concepts.description: A quasi-Newton optimization method that discards the part of the Hessian involving second derivatives, used for fitting projection pursuit models.
concepts.quote: A Gauss–Newton search is convenient for this task. This is a quasi-Newton method, in which the part of the Hessian involving the second derivative of g is discarded.

concepts.name: single hidden layer perceptron
concepts.description: A neural network with one hidden layer, also called a vanilla neural net or single hidden layer back-propagation network.
concepts.quote: Here we describe the most widely used 'vanilla' neural net, sometimes called the single hidden layer back-propagation network, or single layer perceptron.

concepts.name: sigmoid activation function
concepts.description: The nonlinear function σ(v) = 1/(1 + e⁻ᵛ) used to introduce nonlinearity in neural networks.
concepts.quote: The activation function σ(v) is usually chosen to be the sigmoid σ(v) = 1/(1 + e⁻ᵛ).

concepts.name: radial basis function network
concepts.description: A neural network variant that uses Gaussian radial basis functions for the activation function σ(v).
concepts.quote: Sometimes Gaussian radial basis functions (Chapter 6) are used for the σ(v), producing what is known as a radial basis function network.

concepts.name: softmax activation function
concepts.description: An output transformation for K-class classification that produces positive probability estimates summing to one, identical to the multilogit model.
concepts.quote: Early work in K-class classification also used the identity function, but this was later abandoned in favor of the softmax function... This is of course exactly the transformation used in the multilogit model (Section 4.4), and produces positive estimates that sum to one.

concepts.name: hidden units
concepts.description: The middle layer units in a neural network that compute derived features Zm, whose values are not directly observed.
concepts.quote: The units in the middle of the network, computing the derived features Zm, are called hidden units because the values Zm are not directly observed.

concepts.name: back-propagation
concepts.description: A two-pass algorithm for computing gradients in neural networks using the chain rule: forward pass computes predictions, backward pass computes errors and gradients.
concepts.quote: This two-pass procedure is what is known as back-propagation. It has also been called the delta rule (Widrow and Hoff, 1960).

concepts.name: delta rule
concepts.description: Another name for the back-propagation algorithm for training neural networks.
concepts.quote: This two-pass procedure is what is known as back-propagation. It has also been called the delta rule (Widrow and Hoff, 1960).

concepts.name: batch learning
concepts.description: A training approach where parameter updates are computed as a sum over all training cases before updating.
concepts.quote: The updates in (11.13) are a kind of batch learning, with the parameter updates being a sum over all of the training cases.

concepts.name: online learning
concepts.description: A training approach that processes one observation at a time, updating gradients after each training case and cycling through the training set multiple times.
concepts.quote: Learning can also be carried out online—processing each observation one at a time, updating the gradient after each training case, and cycling through the training cases many times.

concepts.name: training epoch
concepts.description: One complete sweep through the entire training set in online learning.
concepts.quote: A training epoch refers to one sweep through the entire training set.

concepts.name: stochastic approximation
concepts.description: A class of iterative methods for optimization where the learning rate decreases to zero as iterations progress, ensuring convergence.
concepts.quote: This learning is a form of stochastic approximation (Robbins and Munro, 1951); results in this field ensure convergence if γr → 0, Σr γr = ∞, and Σr γ²r < ∞.

concepts.name: conjugate gradients
concepts.description: An optimization method that avoids explicit computation of the Hessian while providing faster convergence than back-propagation.
concepts.quote: Better approaches to fitting include conjugate gradients and variable metric methods. These avoid explicit computation of the second derivative matrix while still providing faster convergence.

concepts.name: variable metric methods
concepts.description: Optimization techniques that avoid explicit Hessian computation while achieving faster convergence than gradient descent.
concepts.quote: Better approaches to fitting include conjugate gradients and variable metric methods. These avoid explicit computation of the second derivative matrix while still providing faster convergence.

concepts.name: weight decay
concepts.description: A regularization method for neural networks analogous to ridge regression, adding an L2 penalty on the weights to the error function.
concepts.quote: A more explicit method for regularization is weight decay, which is analogous to ridge regression used for linear models (Section 3.4.1). We add a penalty to the error function R(θ) + λJ(θ).

concepts.name: weight elimination penalty
concepts.description: An alternative regularization penalty that shrinks smaller weights more aggressively than standard L2 weight decay.
concepts.quote: This has the effect of shrinking smaller weights more than (11.16) does.

concepts.name: hinton diagrams
concepts.description: Grayscale heat maps showing estimated neural network weights, used to visualize and interpret what features each hidden unit extracts.
concepts.quote: Figure 11.5 shows heat maps of the estimated weights from the training (grayscale versions of these are called Hinton diagrams.)

concepts.name: local connectivity
concepts.description: A neural network constraint where each hidden unit connects only to a small patch of units in the layer below, reducing total parameters.
concepts.quote: Net-3 uses local connectivity: this means that each hidden unit is connected to only a small patch of units in the layer below.

concepts.name: weight sharing
concepts.description: A constraint where multiple units in a feature map use the same set of weights, forcing extracted features to be computed by the same linear functional across different image regions.
concepts.quote: All units in a local feature map perform the same operation on different parts of the image, achieved by sharing the same weights.

concepts.name: convolutional networks
concepts.description: Neural networks that use local connectivity with shared weights, forcing features to be extracted using the same operation across different spatial locations.
concepts.quote: This forces the extracted features in different parts of the image to be computed by the same linear functional, and consequently these networks are sometimes known as convolutional networks.

concepts.name: feature maps
concepts.description: Arrays of hidden units in convolutional networks that share weights and extract the same type of feature across different regions of the input.
concepts.quote: The first hidden layer of Net-4 has two 8×8 arrays, and each unit takes input from a 3×3 patch just like in Net-3. However, each of the units in a single 8×8 feature map share the same set of nine weights.

concepts.name: receptive field
concepts.description: The region of input that a hidden unit receives connections from in a locally connected network.
concepts.quote: In the first hidden layer (an 8×8 array), each unit takes inputs from a 3×3 patch of the input layer... their receptive fields overlap by one row or column.

concepts.name: bayesian neural networks
concepts.description: Neural networks trained using Bayesian inference with MCMC methods to sample from the posterior distribution of parameters and average predictions.
concepts.quote: Given a prior distribution Pr(θ), the posterior distribution for the parameters is... sophisticated Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods are used to sample from the posterior distribution.

concepts.name: hybrid monte carlo
concepts.description: An MCMC approach that includes an auxiliary momentum vector and implements Hamiltonian dynamics to avoid random walk behavior and achieve faster convergence.
concepts.quote: The particular MCMC approach that was used is called hybrid Monte Carlo, and may be important for the success of the method. It includes an auxiliary momentum vector and implements Hamiltonian dynamics in which the potential function is the target density.

concepts.name: automatic relevance determination
concepts.description: A Bayesian feature selection method where weights for each feature share a common prior variance, and features with small posterior variances are discarded.
concepts.quote: In the latter method (ARD), the weights (coefficients) for the jth feature to each of the first hidden layer units all share a common prior variance σ²j, and prior mean zero. The posterior distributions for each variance σ²j are computed, and the features whose posterior variance concentrates on small values are discarded.

concepts.name: boosted neural networks
concepts.description: Applying boosting to neural networks, typically using weak learners (small networks with few hidden units) as base learners.
concepts.quote: Since boosting is typically most effective with 'weak' learners, we boosted a single hidden layer neural network with two or four units.

concepts.name: bagged neural networks
concepts.description: Applying bootstrap aggregating (bagging) to neural networks by training multiple networks on bootstrap resamples and averaging predictions.
concepts.quote: Bagging perturbs the data in an i.i.d fashion and then re-estimates the model to give a new set of model parameters. At the end, a simple average of the model predictions from different bagged samples is computed.

relationships.quote: Forward stagewise boosting (Algorithm 10.2) is also a very greedy strategy... Thus, the tree predictions T(xi; Θm) are analogous to the components of the negative gradient (10.35).
The components of the negative gradient computed at line 2(a) are referred to as generalized or pseudo residuals, r.

With stochastic gradient boosting (Friedman, 1999), at each iteration we sample a fraction η of the training observations (without replacement), and grow the next tree using that subsample. The rest of the algorithm is identical.
With stochastic gradient boosting (Friedman, 1999), at each iteration we sample a fraction η of the training observations (without replacement).
Controlling the value of M is not the only possible regularization strategy. As with ridge regression and neural networks, shrinkage techniques can be employed as well.
The parameter ν can be regarded as controlling the learning rate of the boosting procedure.
The interaction level of tree-based approximations is limited by the tree size J. Namely, no interaction effects of level greater that J − 1 are possible.
Setting J = 2 (single split 'decision stump') produces boosted models with only main effects; no interactions are permitted.
After the most relevant variables have been identified, the next step is to attempt to understand the nature of the dependence of the approximation f(X) on their joint values.
The function gm(ωᵀmX) is called a ridge function in IRp.

The M = 1 model, known as the single index model in econometrics, is an exception.

The PPR model can approximate any continuous function in IRp arbitrarily well... The neural network model with one hidden layer has exactly the same form as the projection pursuit model.

Sometimes Gaussian radial basis functions (Chapter 6) are used for the σ(v), producing what is known as a radial basis function network.
Early work in K-class classification also used the identity function, but this was later abandoned in favor of the softmax function.

The updates in (11.13) are a kind of batch learning, with the parameter updates being a sum over all of the training cases. Learning can also be carried out online.
A training epoch refers to one sweep through the entire training set.

Often neural networks have too many weights and will overfit the data at the global minimum of R. In early developments of neural networks, either by design or by accident, an early stopping rule was used to avoid overfitting... A more explicit method for regularization is weight decay.
Other forms for the penalty have been proposed, for example... known as the weight elimination penalty. This has the effect of shrinking smaller weights more than (11.16) does.

We decided to compare Bayesian neural networks to boosted trees, boosted neural networks, random forests and bagged neural networks.
Bagging and boosting are non-Bayesian procedures that have some similarity to MCMC in a Bayesian model.
Since boosting is typically most effective with 'weak' learners, we boosted a single hidden layer neural network with two or four units.
For the Bayesian model the w_ℓ = 1/L... For bagging, w_ℓ = 1/L as well... For boosting, the weights are all equal to 1.
